answers.intake_q1: Can you describe the chest pain—where exactly is it located and what does it feel like?
answers.intake_a1: it is sharp and is located next to my sternum on the right
answers.intake_q2: When did the chest pain start and has it changed since then?
answers.intake_a2: 1 week ago and no change since
answers.intake_q3: Any shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, or pain radiating to your arm or jaw?
answers.intake_q4: Have you noticed any swelling, redness, or tenderness in your chest or nearby areas?
answers.intake_q5: Have you had any recent trauma, heavy lifting, or strenuous activity that might have caused the chest tenderness?
answers.intake_q6: Any fever, chills, or recent infections?
answers.intake_q7: Any history of heart disease, blood clots, or lung problems?
answers.intake_q8: Have you tried any treatments or medications for the chest pain, and if so, what was the effect?
answers.intake_q9: Any difficulty breathing when lying flat or swelling in your legs?
answers.intake_summary: Patient presents with one week of sharp, right parasternal chest pain with tenderness following heavy lifting. No associated shortness of breath, radiation, systemic symptoms, or cardiac/pulmonary history. No treatments tried yet.

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with a one-week history of sharp chest pain localized next to the right side of the sternum. The pain is described as sharp and tender to palpation. She reports that the pain began after heavy lifting and has not changed since onset. There are no associated symptoms such as shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, or radiation of pain to the arm or jaw. She denies fever, chills, recent infections, or any history of heart disease, blood clots, or lung problems. No treatments or medications have been tried yet. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no vital signs or physical exam available.

A: 
The working diagnosis is costochondritis, likely related to musculoskeletal strain from heavy lifting, given the localized sharp pain and tenderness over the right parasternal area without systemic or cardiac symptoms. 

Differential: 
1. Costochondritis 
2. Muscular strain or chest wall injury 
3. Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) 
4. Less likely cardiac causes (e.g., angina), given absence of typical features 

P: 
- First-line medication: 
 Ibuprofen 400 mg orally every 6 to 8 hours as needed for pain, for 7 to 10 days. Avoid if history of allergy to NSAIDs or gastrointestinal bleeding. Dosing details were not found in retrieved evidence; prescriber should verify. 

- Alternatives: 
 - Acetaminophen 500 mg to 1000 mg orally every 6 hours as needed for pain, for 7 to 10 days; avoid in severe liver disease. 
 - Naproxen 250 mg to 500 mg orally twice daily as needed for pain, for 7 to 10 days; avoid if allergy to NSAIDs or gastrointestinal bleeding. 

- Medication safety considerations: 
 No known medication allergies. Patient is not pregnant. No renal or liver disease reported. Use NSAIDs with caution if gastrointestinal risk factors develop. 

- Return precautions: 
 Seek immediate care if chest pain worsens, becomes constant, radiates to arm or jaw, or is associated with shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, or syncope. Also return if new swelling, redness, fever, or systemic symptoms develop. 

- Testing: 
 If no improvement within 48–72 hours, worsening symptoms, or recurrent symptoms: seek in-person evaluation for appropriate testing (e.g., urinalysis/urine culture or other workup as indicated).

evidence.title: Heart Disease in Women | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet:

Pain in the neck, jaw, throat, upper belly, or back 
Nausea or vomiting 
Unusual fatigue 
Shortness of breath (feeling like you can't get enough air) during physical activity 
Women who have coronary artery disease are more likely than men to have chest pain when resting or doing daily activities, rather than during exercise. They're also more likely than men to feel chest pain from mental stress.
Symptoms of a heart attack in women may also be different than in men. Chest pain is the most common symptom for both sexes. It may feel like crushing or squeezing. But women are somewhat less likely than men to have chest pa

evidence.title: Turner syndrome - NHS
evidence.snippet:
e typical features or symptoms of the condition are noticed – a blood test can confirm the diagnosis during pregnancy if problems with the heart or kidneys are found during routine ultrasound scans If you're pregnant and your maternity team think your baby could have Turner syndrome, you may be offered tests to check for the condition. This could be either:
chorionic villus sampling (CVS) amniocentesis 
Causes of Turner syndrome 
Turner syndrome is a genetic condition, caused by a missing or partially missing sex chromosome.
It's not possible to prevent Turner syndrome.
Very rarely it can be passed to a child by a parent, but most of the time it happens by chance. It is not linked to age or anything the parents did before or during pregnancy.
How Turner syndrome affects your life 
Turner syndrome can slightly reduce how long someone is expected to live, but most people should be able to

Summary 
Having a pain in your chest can be scary. It does not always mean that you are having a heart attack . There can be many other causes, including:
Other heart problems, such as angina 
Panic attacks 
Digestive problems,

Conditions A to Z
Costochondritis 
Costochondritis is inflammation where your ribs join the bone in the middle of your chest (breastbone). It can cause sharp chest pain, especially when moving or breathing. It usually gets better on its own over time. 
Symptoms of costochondritis 
Costochondritis may cause sharp pain in the front or side of your chest.
The pain may be worse when:
moving your upper body lying down breathing deeply you press the middle of your chest 
Urgent advice: Get help from NHS 111 if:
you think you have costochondritis It is important to get chest pain checked to rule out other more serious conditions.
You can call 111 or get help from 111 online

evidence.title: Congenital heart disease - NHS
evidence.snippet:
face.
These are signs of a serious medical problem.
Information: 
Do not drive yourself to A&E. 
The person you speak to at 999 will give you advice about what to do.
Urgent advice: Ask for an urgent GP appointment or get help from NHS 111 if:
you think you or your child may have symptoms of congenital heart disease You can call 111 or get help from 111 online . Call 111 if you need advice for a child under 5 years old.
Diagnosing congenital heart disease 
Congenital heart disease is screened for during the 20-week pregnancy screening scan . The 20-week scan cannot find all conditions, so it’s possible for congenital heart disease to be misse
